data.title: Why lawsuits won’t cure pandemic’s financial ills between US states and Chinese government
data.description:
In 2025, even as US President Donald Trump sought to stabilise trade ties with Beijing, two Republican-led states were moving in the opposite direction, securing nearly US$50 billion in federal lawsuits for what they say was economic damage caused by China’s deliberate stripping of US hospitals and businesses of essential Covid-19 supplies.
Using a new reading of the “commercial activity” exception to a 50-year-old US law that generally blocks lawsuits against foreign governments, a federal court awarded Missouri US$24 billion in March, saying China’s early-pandemic hoarding of masks and gloves was market behaviour, not a sovereign act.
In November, a federal judge in Mississippi issued a similar US$25 billion judgment based on antitrust and consumer protection claims.
But the year ended with neither state collecting a dime. And what began as a courtroom strategy to hold China accountable has instead morphed into a high-stakes diplomatic tightrope.
Experts say that while attorneys general in both states have vowed to seize Chinese assets, the process is riddled with complex legal barriers that make execution virtually impossible and the dispute has escalated.


With Beijing filing a retaliatory lawsuit in Wuhan, the US State Department has found itself caught in the middle, quietly working to avoid triggering another round of trade tensions as Trump plans his trip to China in April.
William Dodge, an expert in international law at George Washington University’s law school, said that while China has refused to participate in the case, the only remaining option for the states is to serve the judgment on China through diplomatic channels.
“This means Missouri sends the judgment to the State Department. The State Department sends it to the US Embassy in Beijing, and the US Embassy delivers it to the Chinese foreign ministry,” he added.
Describing them as “necessary steps”, Dodge stressed that once the verdict is served, only then can Missouri attempt to enforce the judgment.
“But so far as I know, the US government would not be involved in the process for enforcing a judgment against assets that do not involve the State Department or the US government because they are not involved in the case,” Dodge explained.
The US State Department declined to comment on whether it is assisting the states with enforcement.
China describes US lawsuits as ‘purely malicious litigation’
In a statement to the Post, China’s embassy in Washington described the lawsuits as “purely malicious litigation and political manipulation with extremely sinister intentions”.
Embassy spokesman Liu Pengyu said that “the epidemic prevention and control policies and measures adopted by Chinese governments at all levels fall under the realm of sovereign acts and are not subject to the jurisdiction of US courts”.
Without answering whether the US State Department had been in touch regarding the lawsuits, Pengyu said that China will not accept “any so-called default judgments”, demanding the US side to “return to rationality and correct its mistakes”.
Still, experts warn that if the two states manage to persuade US courts to target Chinese-owned businesses or assets within their borders, the implications could extend far beyond Missouri or Mississippi, potentially opening the door for Beijing to retaliate against American firms anywhere on its soil.
To sue a foreign country, one usually must overcome the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act (FSIA), which shields governments from US lawsuits.
US District Judge Stephen Limbaugh initially dismissed Missouri’s lawsuit in 2022, ruling that the state could not sue China.
But both Missouri and Mississippi advanced a different legal theory, arguing that when China swept up global supplies of personal protective equipment in early 2020, it was downplaying the severity of the virus.
By framing it as predatory market behaviour, the states sought to circumvent the FSIA.
In Missouri, Limbaugh accepted the state’s evidence of US$8 billion in economic damages and applied “treble damages” – multiplying by three – under antitrust law.
In Mississippi, a district judge accepted the state’s calculation that China hoarded exactly 20,836,254 items of PPE. The court fined China US$1,000 per violation, plus damages for the economic impact of more than 4,000 deaths, totalling US$25 billion.
The judge in Missouri also added a 3.91 per cent interest until the judgment is paid.
US lawsuits a clear violation of China’s sovereignty, maintains Beijing
In both cases, China refused to hire lawyers or appear in court, viewing the proceedings as a violation of its sovereignty. This led to an automatic “default judgment” in favour of the states.
The two lawsuits name the Chinese government, the Communist Party of China, several national agencies, provincial and local governments around Wuhan, and the Wuhan Institute of Virology.
Andrew Bailey, who served as Missouri’s Attorney General (AG) until September 2025, said in March that China’s refusal to show up to court didn’t “mean they get away with causing untold suffering and economic devastation”.
“We intend to collect every penny by seizing Chinese-owned assets, including Missouri farmland,” he said.
Specifically, the AG’s office identified around 42,000 acres of agricultural land in northern Missouri currently owned by Smithfield Foods, a subsidiary of the Chinese giant WH Group. While the company has operated in the state for years, state officials argue that, because WH Group is a Chinese entity, its assets could be subject to the enforcement of the judgment.

But Dodge said that while “it’s possible to have a case in which a foreign state can be sued, but if you have a judgment, the judgment still can’t be enforced” due to a second protective shield called “execution immunity”.
“With respect to foreign states, the only property that is not immune from execution is property that is in the US that is used for a commercial activity, and that is used for the commercial activity on which the claim was based,” he noted.
Consequently, Dodge asserted that Chinese-owned property in the US is immune from litigation unless it was explicitly used to hoard personal protective equipment.
As per Chimene Keitner, who served as the Counsellor on International Law in the US Department of State from 2016 to 2017, a Chinese government-owned company’s property is also “not generally available to satisfy a judgment against China”.
She pointed out that only the Wuhan Institute of Virology and the Chinese Academy of Sciences face judgments against them.
“So only their property could be targeted, and only if those defendants are engaged in commercial activities in the United States. If a company wasn’t a defendant in the Missouri lawsuit, its property is not subject to seizure,” Keitner said.


Beijing’s response was swift and severe. Last month, the Intermediate People’s Court of Wuhan accepted a retaliatory lawsuit filed by the Chinese government against the State of Missouri, demanding US$50.5 billion in damages for “reputational harm” and defamation.
The suit explicitly names Catherine Hanaway, who succeeded Bailey as Attorney General, as a defendant, threatening to seize their personal assets if the state proceeds.
Hanaway responded with defiance to the development, saying: “We stand undeterred in our mission to collect on our US$24 billion judgment that was lawfully handed down in federal court. Their threats of ‘reciprocal countermeasures’ are simply an admission of guilt.”
Yet legal scholars say Beijing’s move is less about collecting damages than about signalling how far the dispute could spiral – and how quickly legal brinkmanship could turn into a broader geopolitical confrontation.
Keitner, who’s now at the University of California, Davis, described China’s reciprocal lawsuit “far-fetched”, but added that “under international law, there is a defence called ‘countermeasures’ that lets one country do certain things it couldn’t otherwise do as a way of pressuring another country to comply with international law”.
“So if Missouri violates China’s sovereign immunity, China could have a good argument for doing the same thing not just against Missouri, but against the United States as a whole,” she warned.
Dodge pointed out that the Chinese law also has a “reciprocity provision”, which says that if a foreign state, “like the United States, does not afford the same immunity to China that is provided in China’s law, then China can provide correspondingly less immunity to the foreign state”.
Threat of assets being seized on both sides increases deadlock
He said that if Missouri can convince a US court to disregard the immunity or seize Chinese-owned farmland in the state, “China could start seizing US-owned assets, not just Missouri-owned assets, but any US-owned assets in China to satisfy a judgment that it has”.
Keitner contended that it was unlikely that Missouri would appear in a Chinese court.
“So we’ll likely end up with a default judgment there too,” she explained.
“There also seems to be little chance of an apology from Missouri. Unfortunately, this all seems to be a lot of unhelpful political theatre, with no clear off-ramp for either side to save face.”

Dodge, however, challenged the premise that stockpiling supplies during a crisis constitutes a commercial crime, warning that such a precedent could backfire on the US.
“They [kept] personal protective equipment for people in China and did not sell that personal protective equipment to the rest of the world. Of course, that’s what any government would do when faced with a pandemic. It’s what the United States did with respect to Covid vaccines,” Dodge said.
“So if we start going down the road of saying to governments, ‘if you don’t share your resources broadly, you’re liable to suit’, I think just about every government in the world could be sued.”

data.title: Hourly services at first automated Hong Kong public car park partially resume
data.description:
Hourly services at Hong Kong’s first automated public car park have partially resumed following a suspension of operations for an urgent system update just two days after its debut.
In a reply to the Post, the Architectural Services Department said on Friday that hourly parking services for larger vehicles had resumed at noon, while those for private cars would begin again shortly.
The department said it was aware of the instability of the car parking system on Tong Yin Street in Tseung Kwan O, and had tasked the contractor that built its hardware and software to upgrade the system.
This meant hourly services were suspended from December 31, just two days after the car park debuted. The suspension does not affect monthly parking services.
The department said that before the launch of the car park, the relevant authorities and the contractor had tested the automated parking system using various scenarios.
During the latest system upgrade, the department said the contractor had already assigned additional personnel to support the car park’s operator, which is a separate company.
“The relevant government authorities are closely coordinating with the contractor in hopes that the services will be resumed totally soon,” the department said.

Earlier in the day, notices were put up in the car park to inform users of the suspended services.
“The automated parking system at Tseung Kwan O Government Offices Public Car Park is undergoing an urgent system update,” a notice posted at the site said.
“While monthly parking services are maintained, hourly parking services at the car park are temporarily suspended until further notice.”
The facility provides 388 parking spaces, including 249 for private cars, 25 for motorcycles and 10 for light goods vehicles. It also has 10 bays for light buses, 15 for buses or coaches and 79 for medium or heavy goods vehicles.
The department said the lower-level parking bays for cars and spaces for motorcycles and light goods vehicles were equipped with fee-paying electric vehicle charging facilities.
The car park is fitted with smart equipment, including systems for access control, licence plate recognition with an automatic barrier arm and parking bay information to enhance the experience for users.
About 12 vehicles were parked on the first floor on Friday afternoon, including school buses and trucks, but none were private cars. No vehicles were parked on the ground, second and third floors.
A school bus driver, who declined to be named, put his vehicle on the first floor reserved for monthly parking. He said only the first and second floors were open for monthly parking, and the third floor was closed to the public.

data.title: Trump ‘cancelled’ second attacks on Venezuela after prisoner release, eyes oil investments
data.description:
US President Donald Trump said on Friday he called off a second wave of attacks on Venezuela after the government, whose leftist president was removed by Washington, began releasing prisoners.
Trump made the comments as he prepared to meet major oil corporations that he said will invest at least US$100 billion in Venezuela, which has the world’s largest oil reserves.
Trump was implicitly making clear he is ready again to use force to get his way in Venezuela, which he has vowed to force to enrich the United States, especially oil companies.
“Venezuela is releasing large numbers of political prisoners as a sign of ‘Seeking Peace’,” Trump wrote on his social media platform.
“Because of this cooperation, I have cancelled the previously expected second Wave of Attacks,” he said.
The United States on Friday also announced that it had seized another tanker near Venezuela as it enforces an embargo on its oil, which makes Washington the undisputed power in its key export.


Prisoners’ release
Venezuela began releasing prisoners on Thursday in the first such gesture since US forces removed and detained President Nicolas Maduro in a deadly January 3 raid, with his deputy Delcy Rodriguez taking over.
Former Venezuelan opposition candidate Enrique Marquez – who opposed Maduro in the contested 2024 presidential election – was among those released on Thursday.
“It’s all over now,” Marquez said in a video taken by a local journalist of him and his wife, accompanied by another released opposition member, Biagio Pilieri.
The White House quickly took credit for winning the prisoners’ freedom.
Trump had earlier played down democracy as a motivating factor for the attack, despite years of the United States saying that Maduro was illegitimate and that elections were filled with fraud.
But Trump said he would meet next week with Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado, whom he earlier brushed aside as a “very nice woman” who lacked the “respect” to lead Venezuela.
“I understand she’s coming in next week sometime, and I look forward to saying hello to her,” Trump told Fox News host Sean Hannity in an interview.
Trump had earlier voiced jealousy that Machado won the latest Nobel Peace Prize and said he deserved it.
She has responded by dedicating it to him.




Relatives await releases
Interim leader Rodriguez’s brother, parliament speaker Jorge Rodriguez, said “a large number of Venezuelan and foreign nationals” were being immediately freed for the sake of “peaceful coexistence”.
Renowned Spanish-Venezuelan activist Rocio San Miguel was among five Spanish citizens freed, according to Spain’s foreign ministry.
She was imprisoned since February 2024 over a purported plot to assassinate Maduro, which she denied.
On Tuesday, Trump had told Republican lawmakers that Rodriguez’s administration was closing a “torture chamber” in Caracas but gave no further details.
Venezuelan rights NGO Foro Penal estimates over 800 political prisoners are in the country’s jails.

Venezuela denies being ‘subjugated’
Maduro was seized in a special forces raid accompanied by air strikes, operations that left 100 people dead, according to Caracas.
US forces took Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores to New York to face trial on drugs charges.
Trump said the United States would “run” the Caribbean country for a transitional period and tap into its oil reserves for years.
Delcy Rodriguez insisted on Thursday her country was “not subordinate or subjugated”.
“Nobody surrendered. There was fighting for the homeland” when the US forces attacked, she said during a ceremony for the Venezuelans killed.
Trump announced a plan earlier this week for the United States to sell between 30 million and 50 million barrels of Venezuelan crude, with Caracas then using the money to buy US-made products.
On the streets of Caracas, opinions were mixed.
“I feel we’ll have more opportunities if the oil is in the hands of the United States than in the hands of the government,” said Jose Antonio Blanco, 26.

data.title: US job growth slows amid AI boom and tariff caution, Fed likely to hold rates steady
data.description:
US job growth slowed more than expected in December amid business caution about hiring because of import tariffs and rising artificial intelligence ‌investment, but the unemployment rate dipped to 4.4 per cent, supporting expectations the Federal Reserve would leave interest rates unchanged ‍this month.
Nonfarm payrolls increased by 50,000 jobs last month after rising by a downwardly revised 56,000 in November, the Labor Department’s Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) said on Friday. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast 60,000 jobs added after a previously reported 64,000 increase in November.
The closely watched employment ⁠report suggested the labour market remained stuck in what economists and policymakers have called a “no hire, no fire” mode. It also confirmed the economy was in a jobless expansion. Economic growth and worker productivity surged in the third quarter, in part attributed to the AI spending boom.
The labour market lost considerable momentum last year, largely blamed on US President Donald Trump’s aggressive trade and immigration policies, ‍which economists and policymakers said reduced both demand for and supply of workers.
The sharp moderation in job growth, however, started in 2024. ‍The BLS has estimated about 911,000 fewer jobs were created in the 12 months through March 2025 than previously reported. The agency will publish its payrolls benchmark revision next month with the ‌January employment report.
The overcounting has been blamed on the birth-death model, which is used by the BLS to estimate how many jobs ‍were gained or lost because of companies opening or closing in a given month. Last month, the BLS said it would, starting in January, change the birth-death model by incorporating current sample information each month.

Together with the December employment report, the BLS published annual revisions to the household survey data for the past five years. ‌The ⁠unemployment rate is calculated from the household survey.
The annual population control adjustments, normally incorporated with the January employment report, will be delayed. November’s unemployment rate was revised down to 4.5 per cent from the previously reported 4.6 per cent.
The median forecast in a Reuters poll of economists was for the jobless rate to have eased to 4.5 per cent in December. Some economists say low supply has prevented a ‌sharp rise in the unemployment rate. They estimated that between 50,000 and 120,000 jobs need to be created each month to keep up with growth in the working-age population.
The US central bank cut its benchmark interest rate by ‌a quarter of a percentage point ‌to the 3.50 per cent-3.75 per cent range in December, but officials indicated they were likely to pause further reductions in borrowing costs for now to get a better sense of the economy’s direction.
With factors like tariffs and AI preventing companies from hiring more workers, economists increasingly view the labour market’s challenges as more structural than cyclical, which would make rate cuts less effective to stimulate job growth.

data.title: El Nino could cost Hong Kong US$300 billion and shorten life expectancy: study
data.description:
Extreme weather brought about by the El Nino phenomenon could cost Hong Kong as much as US$300 billion over the 21st century, according to a joint study by local and Singapore universities.
The study also painted a bleak picture for public health, finding that Hongkongers born during the two strong El Nino events in 1982-83 and 1997-98 could have their life expectancy reduced by five to seven months.
Led by Singapore’s Nanyang Technological University in collaboration with City University (CityU), the study analysed more than six decades of mortality records and economic data from 10 high-income Pacific Rim countries and regions.
Published in the scientific journal Nature Climate Change, the study showed that El Nino – a climate pattern involving the warming of the Pacific Ocean – was a “persistent driver of health and economic loss”, not just a short-term weather anomaly.
Researchers estimated life expectancy losses of 0.5 years for the 1982-83 El Nino and 0.4 years for the 1997-98 event.
This is equal to economic losses of US$2.6 trillion for the 1982-83 group and US$4.7 trillion for the 1997-98 batch for all the countries and regions under study.
And intensifying El Nino events could reduce life expectancy by 2.8 years across regions, resulting in economic losses of up to US$35 trillion by the end of the 21st century.

Apart from Hong Kong, the study covered Australia, Canada, Chile, Japan, New Zealand, South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore and the United States.
Corresponding author of the research, Professor Benjamin Horton, dean of CityU’s energy and environment school, said: “While we did not record the loss at a country or region level for future projection, if split by population and economic size, the economic loss for Hong Kong would be around US$250 billion to US$300 billion cumulatively over the 21st century.”
During normal conditions in the Pacific Ocean, trade winds blow west along the equator, taking warm water from South America towards Asia.
To replace that warm water, cold water rises from the depths. El Nino and La Nina are two opposing climate patterns that break these normal conditions.
Scientists call this the El Nino-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle.
El Nino is the warming of the ocean surface in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. Rainfall increases over the tropical Pacific Ocean.
La Nina is the cooling of the ocean surface in the central and eastern tropical Pacific Ocean. Rainfall decreases over the central tropical Pacific Ocean.
According to the study, El Nino can threaten human health, increasing mortality during event years. This includes infectious diseases, cardiovascular and respiratory illnesses. Children and the elderly face higher risks.
“For Hong Kong, our best estimate of the loss in life expectancy at birth associated with the 1997-98 event is 0.4 years, corresponding to an economic loss of about US$58 billion for the total population,” Horton said regarding past El Nino events.
“The estimated impact of the 1982-83 event is a life expectancy at birth loss of around 0.6 years, equivalent to approximately US$15 billion [for the total population at the time].”
He called for incorporating El Nino-related health risks into long-term planning.
“Mitigation alone will not protect us. Targeted adaptation is essential.
“To adapt to El Nino, countries need strong early-warning systems, heat-health action plans, better water management, and protection for workers exposed to extreme heat,” Horton said.
“We also need resilient infrastructure, smarter agriculture that can cope with drought or heavy rainfall, and public-health systems prepared for spikes in disease and pollution.
“El Nino is predictable. So with the right planning, we can reduce its impacts.”
A co-author of the paper, Dr Dhrubajyoti Samanta, a senior research fellow at Nanyang Technological University’s Earth Observatory of Singapore, said: “Our study shows that ENSO’s influence extends far beyond short-term weather disruptions.
“As ENSO events intensify in a warming world, their long-lasting effects on population health become increasingly evident, emphasising the critical role of climate variability in shaping societal outcomes.
“These scientific insights also indicate that understanding and anticipating ENSO-driven risks will be increasingly important for early warning systems and long-term planning.”

data.title: The great chip leap: China’s semiconductor equipment self-reliance surges past targets
data.description:
China’s drive for chip manufacturing equipment self-sufficiency advanced so rapidly in 2025 that even the country’s planners were caught by surprise, as the ratio of domestically developed semiconductor equipment surged to 35 per cent by the year’s end, up from 25 per cent in 2024.
The ratio was higher than Beijing’s target of 30 per cent, set in early 2025 to encourage China’s semiconductor industry to favour local suppliers over US rivals such as Applied Materials, Lam Research and KLA, according to a report by Jiemian News, a Chinese media outlet.
Progress had been particularly evident in critical segments such as etching and thin-film deposition, where adoption of local equipment had surpassed 40 per cent, thanks to the progress of local manufacturers such as Naura Technology Group and Advanced Micro-Fabrication Equipment.
The 5-nanometre-grade etching machine made by Advanced Micro-Fabrication had entered validation for Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co’s advanced process lines, according to the report.
Oxidation and diffusion furnaces from Naura accounted for more than 60 per cent of the 28nm production lines at Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corp, China’s top foundry. Naura’s order backlog now extended into the first quarter of 2027.

Piotech doubled its share of plasma-enhanced chemical vapour deposition equipment at Yangtze Memory Technology’s 3D NAND production lines, bringing the company’s equipment share to 30 per cent from 15 per cent, according to the report.
China has been working hard to reduce its reliance on imported chip equipment, particularly from the US, since Washington imposed export restrictions in 2022. Chinese developers of new semiconductor lines are explicitly required to source at least 50 per cent of tooling domestically.
China was projected to maintain its position as the world’s largest market for semiconductor equipment through 2027, boosted by continuous capital investment in both legacy and advanced manufacturing nodes, according to industry association SEMI.
Chinese investors are putting money into key suppliers of chip equipment. Piotech’s share price in Shanghai has jumped 150 per cent over the past year, while Naura gained 75 per cent and Advanced Micro-Fabrication surged 85 per cent.
China is encouraging the development of domestic chip equipment through financing support from the China Integrated Circuit Industry Investment Fund, known as the “Big Fund”. Its third phase recently announced a 2 billion yuan (US$286 million) investment into the sector, specifically targeting research and development of core components for etchers and lithography systems.
The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology was also offering procurement subsidies of up to 15 per cent to companies that adopt domestic equipment in their wafer fabrication production lines, according to the report.

data.title: EU agrees to ‘world’s largest’ free-trade zone with Mercosur pact after 25 years
data.description:
A majority of European Union countries gave the go-ahead on Friday for the bloc to sign the Mercosur free trade deal with Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay after decades of negotiations.
At a meeting of representatives from the 27 EU countries in Brussels, a sufficient number of participants agreed to the planned signing of the deal, diplomatic sources said.
The new free-trade zone, with more than 700 million inhabitants, will be the largest of its kind in the world, according to the European Commission. The controversial deal has been in the making for more than 25 years.
The final agreement was made possible by last-minute concessions to Europe’s agricultural lobby, which views competition from the four countries – all members of the wider Mercosur South American trading bloc – as a threat to European farmers.
In South America, agricultural products such as beef can be produced more cheaply than in Europe.

Overcoming political hurdles
The agreement was initially supposed to be signed in Brazil in December. However, the necessary majority of EU member states was not achieved at that time because Italy tied its approval to further support commitments for European farmers.
For the deal to pass, it required a so-called qualified majority, meaning at least 15 of the EU’s 27 states representing 65 per cent of the bloc’s population must back it.
Italy’s approval was necessary because two other populous EU countries, France and Poland, oppose the agreement in its current form.
The new free trade area is also intended as a signal offsetting US President Donald Trump’s protectionist trade policy. The aim is to dismantle trade and tariff barriers between the EU and the Mercosur states as much as possible.

Economic boost and German support
German industry had been pushing for years for the deal to be concluded.
German Chancellor Friedrich Merz welcomed Friday’s breakthrough as a “milestone in European trade policy and an important signal of our strategic sovereignty and ability to act”.
“With this agreement, we are strengthening our economy and trade relations with our partners in South America – that is good for Germany and for Europe,” he added.
The commission estimates that the agreement could increase annual EU exports to South America by up to 39 per cent, or around €49 billion (US$57 billion) – supporting more than 440,000 jobs throughout Europe.
In particular, the automotive industry, mechanical engineering and the pharmaceutical sector stand to benefit. Car imports to Mercosur countries are currently subject to a 35 per cent tariff, for example.
Negotiations on the Mercosur agreement began back in 1999. The commission finally concluded the talks in December 2024, despite persistent criticism from countries such as France.
Last year, the EU’s trade dispute with the United States brought new momentum to the process. Many countries, and Germany especially, now want to demonstrate that fair trade is not a thing of the past.
“Now it is important to conclude the next free trade agreements quickly,” Merz stressed, noting that 25 years of negotiations had been too long.

Safety clauses and safeguards
However, critics of the deal fear that European farmers could be forced into a merciless price war, while fuelling the destruction of the South American rainforest. There are also concerns that strict EU standards on food safety and animal and plant protection could be undermined.
The commission in Brussels firmly denies this, stressing that only products complying with the bloc’s extensive regulations can be imported into the EU.
To counter ongoing farmers’ protests against the planned free trade area, the commission recently drew up additional economic safety clauses.
That includes countermeasures that can be quickly initiated in the event of a harmful increase in imports from the Mercosur states or an excessive fall in prices for EU producers. These would lead to the temporary suspension of trade advantages.
The imports of products including beef, poultry, rice, honey, eggs, garlic, ethanol, citrus fruits and sugar are to be monitored particularly closely. A report on the impact of imports from Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay is to be published at least every six months.
Bolivia, which is now also part of Mercosur, has to adapt its rules before it can benefit from the free trade agreement.
The agreement is now to be signed in Paraguay, which currently holds Mercosur’s rotating presidency. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and European Council President António Costa are to attend the signing ceremony.
A written decision based on Friday’s agreement is now required to sign and finalise the trade deal. This is considered a formality, however.

data.title: US House lawmakers abandon bid to bring back controversial China Initiative
data.description:
Following months of opposition from the Asian-American community, US House lawmakers on Thursday removed a provision from a bill aimed at restarting the China Initiative, a programme that unfairly targeted Chinese-American researchers.
Last September, the proposal to “direct the re-establishment of the China Initiative” was included as a measure to “counter China and maintain America’s competitive edge” in the financial year 2026 appropriations bill approved by the House Committee on Appropriations.
Gisela Perez Kusakawa, executive director of the Asian American Scholar Forum, on Thursday commended the removal, saying efforts to revive the US Justice Department’s China Initiative “undermine trust, harm American research and disproportionately endanger Asian-American scholars, researchers and scientists”.
“The removal of this language is an important affirmation that national security policies must be rooted in evidence, fairness and respect for American values and freedoms,” Perez Kusakawa said.


John Yang, president of Asian Americans Advancing Justice, said anti-Asian hate levels in the US remained at historic highs.
“We are encouraged to see that Congress listened to our concerns and removed language that sought to unfairly target Chinese-American and Chinese immigrant scientists, researchers and academics under the guise of national security,” Yang said.
The two groups were among 82 civil rights and Asian-American advocacy organisations that signed a letter sent to the influential House committee in September last year opposing the language.
The letter said that “reinstating the ‘China Initiative’ or any similar programme would … exacerbate the existing chilling effect, discouraging talented scholars and scientists from contributing to US scientific innovation and may result in significant loss of talent”.
“This would hamper America’s scientific progress and innovation, and hurt our country’s ability to remain a global leader in science and technology to the detriment of all,” it added.
Launched in 2018 during US President Donald Trump’s first term, the China Initiative was touted as combating suspected economic espionage but came to be widely criticised as racially biased and ineffective.
The Justice Department officially ended the programme in 2022 following a series of failed prosecutions and a backlash from the scientific community.
Remaining provisions in the 2026 appropriations bill outlined a strategy to counter China through investment in emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence, quantum computing and advanced manufacturing. It also backed the Artemis lunar exploration programme and efforts to counter “China’s malign ambitions to steal American research”.
In recent years, the US and China have been locked in a mounting rivalry for technological and scientific supremacy, competing in critical and emerging fields such as AI, semiconductors, quantum science and biotechnology.
Leading scientists have also been relocating from the US to China in a wave that is altering the landscape of global research, with some citing funding cuts, unfavourable research conditions, politics and family reasons.

data.title: Jerry Shang casts doubt on Hong Kong return, as Kazakh conqueror rues loss of easy matches
data.description:
Alexander Bublik said he missed “the easy matches” from the professional tour of five years ago, after he resisted a second-set fightback from world No 406 Jerry Shang Juncheng in their Bank of China Hong Kong Tennis Open quarter-final on Friday.
The Kazakh won 6-1, 7-6 in one hour and 25 minutes to book a semi-final clash with Marcos Giron of America.
Bublik raced into a 5-0 lead and, after Shang finally got on the board following a sixth game of six deuces, wrapped up the set in 28 minutes.
Shang, who later raised the possibility that he might skip Hong Kong next year, was suffering from a sore throat and called for medication early in the second set. He increased his level, but Bublik saved a set point on his own serve at 5-6, before speeding through the tiebreak for the loss of only two points.
Bublik, who will enter the world’s top 10 should he claim his ninth career title this weekend, acknowledged it was “not good for me” that the men’s tour talent pool had grown so deep.
“I prefer easy matches, I like the tennis that it was five years ago when you had a bunch of random people playing,” he said.
“Now it doesn’t matter who you play; a guy [ranked] 300 in the world, a guy top 10, it’s still the same level, besides the main two guys [Carlos Alcaraz and Jannik Sinner]. I prefer easy matches.”

Bublik said he had “played well and solidly, and delivered the best shots I could in the most important moments.
“Playing a guy I know well and respect a lot, I knew he was going to be clutch,” he said.
Illness forced Shang to withdraw from his semi-final against Kei Nishikori last year.
Under the weather again 12 months later, he said: “Overall, I’m feeling OK, but 13 degrees doesn’t help anyone feel healthy. He was just the better player, so no complaints.”
Also a semi-finalist in 2024, Shang said Hong Kong had provided “a lot of positive things”.
He also thanked organisers for awarding him a wild card after an injury-hit 2025 led to his ranking tumbling from a career-high world No 47 in October 2024.
Nonetheless, the 20-year-old stopped short of guaranteeing his return for a fourth edition in 2027, and mooted the prospect of instead competing in the United Cup, the early-season mixed-team tournament in Australia.
“It could be something to look forward to … to represent my country and have the flag on from the beginning of the year,” Shang said. “It could be a fun idea for me next year, but I get a lot of support here, so I’ll see what the year has for me.”
Giron, who beat defending champion Alexandre Muller in round two, advanced to meet Bublik after fellow American Michael Mmoh withdrew injured when he was trailing 6-3, 1-0 following 49 minutes on court.

data.title: Hong Kong students lauded for using AI to help elderly fulfil travel dreams
data.description:
A group of Hong Kong students has created an award-winning initiative that uses artificial intelligence to help elderly couples fulfil their travel dreams, with the city leader’s wife highlighting the technology’s role in volunteer services.
Janet Lee Lam Lai-sim, the spouse of Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, lauded the achievement at the Hong Kong Volunteer Award’s presentation ceremony on Friday, saying innovation and technology would drive progress in volunteer development.
“I hope everyone can leverage technology to break through constraints of time and geography, so that people of all ages and backgrounds can participate in voluntary work, and together unleash the spirit of caring for Hong Kong,” she said.
The award is co-organised by the Home and Youth Affairs Bureau and the government-funded Agency for Volunteer Service.
At the ceremony, agency chairwoman Melissa Kaye Pang also thanked volunteers for their role in supporting victims of the recent fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, saying their efforts “demonstrated the most precious aspect of the volunteer spirit”.
Other cases highlighted at the event included the work of students from Buddhist Sum Heung Lam Memorial College in Tuen Mun, whose project allowed elderly people, including those with cognitive impairments, to take pictures using AI-generated backgrounds of places they were unable to visit.
“For instance, a couple wanted to visit Brazil but couldn’t due to their physical condition, so we used AI technology to help them realise their wish,” said Chow Wah, the teacher supervising the project. “It’s moving for them and deeply moving for us too.”

The group won the 2025 Jockey Club Volunteers Together Outstanding Caring School Award for their work.
Chow said the team completed 12 photo shoots last year as part of the project and had also learned that elderly people needed more “mental support”.
Student volunteer Chen Yui-nam, who handled photography for the team, echoed his teacher’s comments.
“I realised the elderly need attention not just physically, but mentally,” the 18-year-old said.
Other young volunteers used their various tools and talents to support members of the community, including Cheryl Lau Tsz-wing.
The 17-year-old student from Heep Yunn School led a band that performed at public hospitals for psychiatric patients, winning the Jockey Club Volunteers Together Outstanding Rising Star Volunteer Award for their efforts.
The band played songs requested by patients and encouraged them to sing along.
“Patients didn’t choose to be ill. They feel lonely and unhappy. The lyrics and melody can make them happy, feel warm and temporarily forget the pain of illness,” said Lau, who has been volunteering for more than two years.
“Seeing them leave with smiles makes us very happy.”
